From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Highest division of the National Collegiate Athletic Association

For defending NCAA Division I champions, see [List of current NCAA Division I champions](/wiki/List_of_current_NCAA_Division_I_champions "List of current NCAA Division I champions").

[![NCAA Division I logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/NCAA_DI_logo_c.svg/250px-NCAA_DI_logo_c.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NCAA_DI_logo_c.svg "NCAA Division I logo")

NCAA Division I logo

| [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") divisions |
| --- |
| Division I  [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II")  [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:NCAA_divisions "Template:NCAA divisions") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:NCAA_divisions "Template talk:NCAA divisions") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:NCAA_divisions "Special:EditPage/Template:NCAA divisions") |

**NCAA Division I** (**D-I**) is the highest division of [intercollegiate athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics") sanctioned by the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), which accepts players globally. D-I schools include the major collegiate athletic powers, with large budgets, more elaborate facilities and more [athletic scholarships](/wiki/Athletic_scholarship "Athletic scholarship") than [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") and [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") as well as many smaller schools committed to the highest level of intercollegiate competition.

This level was previously called the University Division of the NCAA, in contrast to the lower-level College Division; these terms were replaced with [numeric](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals") divisions in 1973. The University Division was renamed Division I, while the College Division was split in two; the College Division members that offered scholarships or wanted to compete against those who did became Division II, while those who did not want to offer scholarships became Division III.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

For [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") only, D-I schools are further divided into the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS), the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (FCS), and those institutions that do not have any football program. FBS teams have more players receiving athletic scholarships than FCS teams and until 2024, had minimum game-attendance requirements. The FBS is named for its series of postseason [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_games "Bowl games"), with various polls ranking teams after the conclusion of these games, while the FCS national champion is determined by a multi-team bracket tournament.

For the 2020–21 school year, Division I contained 357 of the NCAA's 1,066 member institutions, with 130 in the Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS), 127 in the Football Championship Subdivision (FCS), and 100 non-football schools, with six additional schools in the transition from Division II to Division I.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) There was a moratorium on any additional movement up to D-I until 2012, after which any school that wants to move to D-I must be accepted for membership by a conference and show the NCAA it has the financial ability to support a D-I program.

## Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Finances")]

Division I athletic programs generated $8.7 billion in revenue in the 2009–10 academic year. Men's teams provided 55%, women's teams 15%, and 30% was not categorized by sex or sport. Football and men's basketball are usually a university's only profitable sports,[[4]](#cite_note-thomas20110426-4) and are called "revenue sports".[[5]](#cite_note-mandel20140623-5) From 2008 to 2012, 205 varsity teams were dropped in NCAA Division I – 72 for women and 133 for men, with men's tennis, gymnastics and wrestling hit particularly hard.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (130 schools in 2017), between 50 and 60 percent of football and men's basketball programs generated positive revenues (above program expenses).[[7]](#cite_note-7) However, in the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (124 schools in 2017), only four percent of football and five percent of men's basketball programs generated positive revenues.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 2012, 2% of athletic budgets were spent on equipment, uniforms and supplies for male athletes at NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision school, with the median spending per-school at $742,000.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 2014, the NCAA and the student athletes debated whether student athletes should be paid. In April, the NCAA approved students-athletes receiving free unlimited meals and snacks. The NCAA stated "The adoption of the meals legislation finished a conversation that began in the Awards, Benefits, Expenses and Financial Aid Cabinet. Members have worked to find appropriate ways to ensure student-athletes get the nutrition they need without jeopardizing Pell Grants or other federal aid received by the neediest student-athletes. With their vote, members of the council said they believe loosening NCAA rules on what and when food can be provided from athletics departments is the best way to address the issue."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

According to the finance section of the NCAA page, "The NCAA receives most of its annual revenue from two sources: television and marketing rights for the Division I Men's Basketball Championship and ticket sales for all championships. That money is distributed in more than a dozen ways — almost all of which directly support NCAA schools, conferences and nearly half a million student-athletes. About 60% of the NCAA's annual revenue — around $600 million — is annually distributed directly to Division I member schools and conferences, while more than $150 million funds Division I championships" (NCAA 2021).

## Football conferences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Football conferences")]

Under NCAA regulations, all Division I conferences defined as "multisport conferences" must meet the following criteria:[[11]](#cite_note-MultisportConf-11)

* A total of at least seven active Division I members. However, the NCAA's Grace Period rule (Bylaw 20.02.9.2) allows conferences to operate for up to two years with less than the minimum.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* Separate from the above, at least seven active Division 1 members that sponsor both men's and women's basketball.
* Sponsorship of at least 12 NCAA Division I sports.
* Minimum of six men's sports, with the following additional restrictions:
  + Men's basketball is a mandatory sport, and at least seven members must sponsor that sport.
  + Non-football conferences must sponsor at least two men's team sports other than basketball.
  + At least six members must sponsor five men's sports other than basketball, including either football or two other team sports.
* Minimum of six women's sports, with the following additional restrictions:
  + Women's basketball is a mandatory sport, with at least seven members sponsoring that sport.
  + At least two other women's team sports must be sponsored.
  + At least six members must sponsor five women's sports other than basketball, with at least two of those five being team sports. If a conference officially sponsors an [NCAA "emerging sport" for women](/wiki/NCAA_Emerging_Sports_for_Women "NCAA Emerging Sports for Women") (as of 2023–24, acrobatics & tumbling, equestrianism, rugby union, stunt, triathlon, or wrestling), that sport will be counted if five members (instead of six) sponsor it.

### FBS conferences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: FBS conferences")]

FBS conferences must meet a more stringent set of requirements for NCAA recognition than other conferences:[[13]](#cite_note-13)

* A total of at least eight active FBS members.
* To be counted toward this total, a school must participate in conference play in at least six men's and eight women's sports, including men's and women's basketball, football, and at least two other women's team sports.
  + Each school may count one men's and one women's sport not sponsored by its primary conference toward the above limits, as long as that sport competes in another Division I conference. The men's and women's sports so counted need not be the same sport.[[14]](#cite_note-NCAA_Division_Differences-14)

The non-FBS schools of the original conference left to form a new conference that purchased [the Big East name](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference"), while the FBS schools continued to operate under the old Big East's charter and structure. Notes:

:   The NCAA officially classifies the men's championships in volleyball and water polo as "National Collegiate" championships, that being the designation for championships that are open to members of more than one NCAA division. The ice hockey championship, however, is styled as a "Division I" championship because of the previous existence of a separate Division II championship in that sport.

* Football — D-I football programs are divided into FBS and FCS. The 133 FBS programs can award financial aid to as many as 85 players, with each player able to receive up to a full scholarship. The 128 FCS programs can award up to the equivalent of 63 full scholarships, divided among no more than 85 individuals. Some FCS conferences restrict scholarships to a lower level or prohibit scholarships altogether.
* Soccer — As of the most recent 2023 NCAA soccer season (part of the 2023–24 academic year), four of the 10 FBS conferences do not sponsor men's soccer: the Big 12, Conference USA, Mid-American Conference, and the SEC. Several other D-I conferences also do not sponsor the sport: the Big Sky, MEAC, Mountain West, Southland, and SWAC. The Ohio Valley Conference is the most recent conference to add men's soccer; it started sponsoring the sport for the first time in the 2023 season. Conference USA shut down its league after losing most of its men's soccer membership to the Sun Belt Conference, followed by the American Athletic Conference taking in CUSA's remaining four teams (three of which fully joined The American in 2023) as associate members for 2022. The MAC was reduced to 5 men's soccer members in the 2022 season, and shut down its league at the end of that season after being unable to find the sixth member needed to maintain its automatic NCAA tournament bid. Of its final men's soccer members, three moved that sport to the Missouri Valley Conference, one to the Big East Conference, and one to the Ohio Valley Conference.
  + The Pac-12 will be reduced to two full members in 2024, with only Oregon State sponsoring men's soccer. Oregon State will house most of its non-football sports in the West Coast Conference in the 2024–25 and 2025–26 school years. Pac-12 men's soccer affiliate San Diego State will move that sport to the Western Athletic Conference.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
* Ice hockey — Almost all D-I ice hockey programs are in the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"), or the [Colorado Front Range](/wiki/Front_Range_Urban_Corridor "Front Range Urban Corridor"). Only one D-I all-sports conference, the [Big Ten](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"), sponsors a men's hockey league. All other conferences operate as hockey-specific leagues. Of the 61 teams competing in D-I hockey in 2022–23, 22 are otherwise classified as either D-II or D-III; a number of schools from D-II play in D-I ice hockey as the NCAA no longer sponsors a championship in D-II and many have traditional/cultural fan bases that support ice hockey, and the D-III schools were "grandfathered" in to D-I through their having sponsored hockey prior to the creation of D-III.
* Lacrosse — The vast majority of D-I lacrosse programs are from the Northeast, Southeast, and [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states "Mid-Atlantic states"). Only five D-I programs are not in the [Eastern Time Zone](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"): [Air Force](/wiki/Air_Force_Falcons_men%27s_lacrosse "Air Force Falcons men's lacrosse") and [Denver](/wiki/Denver_Pioneers_men%27s_lacrosse "Denver Pioneers men's lacrosse") on the [Colorado Front Range](/wiki/Front_Range_urban_corridor "Front Range urban corridor"), [Lindenwood](/wiki/Lindenwood_Lions "Lindenwood Lions") on the Missouri side of the [St. Louis metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), [Marquette](/wiki/Marquette_Golden_Eagles_men%27s_lacrosse "Marquette Golden Eagles men's lacrosse") in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah_Utes_men%27s_lacrosse "Utah Utes men's lacrosse"). Lindenwood will drop men's lacrosse after the 2024 season (2023–24 school year).[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
* Volleyball — Of the traditional D-I conferences, only the [Big West Conference](/wiki/Big_West_Conference "Big West Conference") and [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") sponsor men's volleyball, with those conferences respectively adding the sport in 2017–18 and 2022–23. Two of the other three major volleyball conferences, defined in that sport as leagues that include full Division I members, are volleyball-specific conferences; the third is the [Mountain Pacific Sports Federation](/wiki/Mountain_Pacific_Sports_Federation "Mountain Pacific Sports Federation"), a multi-sport conference that does not sponsor football or basketball. In addition to the D-I schools, 33 D-II schools are competing in the National Collegiate division in 2023–24; eight of these are members of [Conference Carolinas](/wiki/Conference_Carolinas "Conference Carolinas"), the first all-sports league outside Division III to sponsor the sport; six are members of the [Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Southern_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Conference "Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Conference"); and four are either full or affiliate members of the [East Coast Conference](/wiki/East_Coast_Conference "East Coast Conference"), which began sponsoring the sport in 2023–24.
* Water Polo — The number of D-I schools sponsoring men's water polo has declined from 35 in 1987/88 to 22 in 2010/11.[[27]](#cite_note-76) No school outside of California has ever made the finals of the championship, and all champions since 1998 have come from one of the four California schools that are leaving the Pac-12 in 2024.

## Broadcasting and revenue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Broadcasting and revenue")]

NCAA Division I schools have broadcasting contracts that showcase their more popular sports — typically football and men's basketball — on network television and in basic cable channels. These contracts can be quite lucrative, particularly for D-I schools from the biggest conferences. For example, the Big Ten conference in 2016 entered into contracts with Fox and ESPN that pay the conference $2.64 billion over six years.

The NCAA also holds certain TV contracts. For example, the NCAA's contract to show the men's basketball championship tournament (widely known as March Madness) is currently under a 14-year deal with CBS and Turner that runs from 2010 to 2024 and pays $11 billion.

For the 2023–24 fiscal year, the conferences that earned the most revenues (and that distributed the most revenues to each of their member schools) were:

1. Big 10 — $928 million (dispersed $63.2 million each)
2. SEC — $840 million (dispersed $50.5 million to each of its member schools)
3. Big 12 — $558 million (dispersed $34.8 million each)
4. ACC — $487 million (dispersed $28 million each)
5. Pac-12 — N/A

## Scholarship and roster limits by sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Scholarship and roster limits by sport")]

### Old rules pre-July 1, 2025

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Old rules pre-July 1, 2025")]

Through the 2024–25 academic year, the NCAA had limits on the total financial aid each Division I member could award in each sport that the school sponsors. It divided sports that are sponsored into two types for purposes of scholarship limitations:

* "Head-count" sports, in which the NCAA limits the total number of individuals that can receive athletic scholarships, but allows each player to receive up to a full scholarship.
* "Equivalency" sports, in which the NCAA limits the total financial aid that a school can offer in a given sport to the equivalent of a set number of full scholarships. Roster limitations may or may not apply, depending on the sport.

The term "counter" was also key to this concept. The NCAA defines a "counter" as "an individual who is receiving institutional financial aid that is countable against the aid limitations in a sport."[[42]](#cite_note-91)

### New rules since July 1, 2025

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: New rules since July 1, 2025")]

Following the 2024 settlement of the *[House v. NCAA](/wiki/House_v._NCAA "House v. NCAA")* legal case, scholarship limits were replaced by roster limits in all sports. These limits, which took effect in 2025–26, apply to members of conferences that were defendants in *House* (the ["Power Four" conferences](/wiki/Power_conferences "Power conferences") and the [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference"), which had "power" status before its 2024 collapse), plus programs that opted into the *House* framework.[[63]](#cite_note-121)

Student-athletes who had been on NCAA teams before 2025–26 and had athletic eligibility remaining were granted a blanket exemption from roster limits. The NCAA created a database of such individuals, and anyone listed in that database can be on a roster for the remainder of their eligibility without counting against roster limits.[[64]](#cite_note-122)

### Rules for multi-sport athletes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Rules for multi-sport athletes")]

The NCAA also has rules specifying the sport in which multi-sport athletes are to be counted, with the basic rules being:[[65]](#cite_note-Multisport-124)

* Anyone who participates in football is counted in that sport, even if he does not receive financial aid from the football program. An exception exists for players at non-scholarship FCS programs who receive aid in another sport.[[66]](#cite_note-125)
* Participants in basketball are counted in that sport, unless they also play football.
* Participants in men's ice hockey are counted in that sport, unless they also play football or basketball.
* Participants in both men's swimming and diving and men's water polo are counted in swimming and diving, unless they count in football or basketball.
* Participants in women's (indoor) volleyball are counted in that sport unless they also play basketball.
* All other multi-sport athletes are counted in whichever sport the school chooses.

## Football subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Football subdivisions")]

Subdivisions in Division I exist only in [football](/wiki/College_football "College football").[[67]](#cite_note-usatoday.com-126)[[68]](#cite_note-127) In all other sports, all Division I conferences are equivalent. The subdivisions were recently given names to reflect the differing levels of football play in them.

As of the 2023 season, the main distinctions between Bowl Subdivision and Championship Subdivision schools are scholarship policies and the existence of an official NCAA championship in the latter subdivision.[[69]](#cite_note-FBSreq23-128)[[70]](#cite_note-Bowl_Subdivision_Requirements-129) Before the 2023 season, the NCAA required that FBS schools average at least 15,000 attendance, allowing schools to report either total tickets sold or the number of persons in attendance at the games. The requirement was a minimum average of 15,000 people in attendance every other year.[[70]](#cite_note-Bowl_Subdivision_Requirements-129) These numbers are posted to the NCAA statistics website for football each year. With new rules starting in the 2006 season, it was possible for the number of Bowl Subdivision schools to drop in the future if those schools were not able to pull in enough fans into the games. Additionally, 14 FCS schools had enough attendance to be moved up in 2012.[[71]](#cite_note-130) Under current NCAA rules, these schools must have an invitation from an FBS conference in order to move to FBS. The difference in the postseasons in each of the subdivisions grant the FCS an advantage to have the best record in college football history, 17–0, while the FBS allows a 16–0 record with the playoff expansion in the 2024 season.

FBS attendance requirements were abolished early in the 2023 season, effective immediately. In their place, Division I added new requirements for athletic funding. Effective in 2027–28, FBS schools must fund the equivalent of at least 210 full scholarships across all of their NCAA sports; spend at least $6 million annually on athletic scholarships; and provide at least 90% of the total number of allowed scholarship equivalents across 16 sports, including football.[[69]](#cite_note-FBSreq23-128)

### Football Bowl Subdivision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Football Bowl Subdivision")]

Main article: [NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision")

See also: [College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") and [List of NCAA Division I FBS football bowl records](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_I_FBS_football_bowl_records "List of NCAA Division I FBS football bowl records")

**Division I Football Bowl Subdivision** (**FBS**), formerly known as **Division I-A**, is the top level of [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"). Schools in Division I FBS compete in post-season [bowl games](/wiki/List_of_college_bowl_games "List of college bowl games"), with the champions of five conferences, along with the highest-ranked champion of the other five conferences, receiving [automatic bids](/wiki/Automatic_Qualifying_conference "Automatic Qualifying conference") to the [access bowls](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff#Bowl_selections "College Football Playoff").

Before the *House* settlement, FBS schools were limited to a total of 85 football players receiving financial assistance.[[72]](#cite_note-131) For competitive reasons, a student receiving partial scholarship counted fully against the total of 85. Nearly all FBS schools that are not on NCAA probation gave 85 full scholarships. The *House* settlement replaced the 85-scholarship limit with a 105-player roster limit.

As of the current 2025 college football season, there are 134 full members of Division I FBS, plus two transitional schools that are considered FBS members for scheduling purposes. The newest full FBS member is [Kennesaw State](/wiki/Kennesaw_State_Owls_football "Kennesaw State Owls football"), which completed a transition from FCS prior to the [2025 season](/wiki/2025_NCAA_Division_I_FBS_football_season "2025 NCAA Division I FBS football season"). The next schools to become full FBS members are [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_Fightin%27_Blue_Hens_football "Delaware Fightin' Blue Hens football") and [Missouri State](/wiki/Missouri_State_Bears_football "Missouri State Bears football"), which joined [Conference USA](/wiki/Conference_USA "Conference USA") in 2025 and will become full FBS members in 2026.[[15]](#cite_note-MO_CUSA-17)

Since the [2016 season](/wiki/2016_NCAA_Division_I_FBS_football_season "2016 NCAA Division I FBS football season"), all FBS conferences have been allowed to conduct a championship game that does not count against the limit of 12 regular-season contests. Under the current rules, most recently changed in advance of the 2022 season, conferences have complete freedom to determine the participants in their championship games.[[73]](#cite_note-132) From 2016 to 2021, FBS rules allowed such a game to be held either (1) between the winners of each of two divisions, with each team having played a full round-robin schedule within its division, or (2) between the conference's top two teams after a full round-robin conference schedule.[[74]](#cite_note-133) Before 2016, "exempt" championship games could only be held between the divisional winners of conferences that had at least 12 football teams and split into divisions.[[75]](#cite_note-134)[[76]](#cite_note-135) The prize is normally a specific bowl game bid for which the conference has a tie-in.

Some conferences have numbers in their names but this often has no relation to the number of member institutions in the conference. The Big Ten Conference did not formally adopt the "Big Ten" name until 1987, but unofficially used that name when it had 10 members from 1917 to 1946, and again from 1949 forward. However, it has continued to use the name even after it expanded to 11 members with the addition of Penn State in 1990, 12 with the addition of Nebraska [in 2011](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932014_Big_Ten_Conference_realignment#Nebraska "2010–2014 Big Ten Conference realignment"), and 14 with the arrival of Maryland and Rutgers [in 2014](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932014_Big_Ten_Conference_realignment#Maryland "2010–2014 Big Ten Conference realignment"). The Big 12 Conference was established in 1996 with 12 members, but continues to use that name even after a [number of departures and a few replacements](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932013_Big_12_Conference_realignment "2010–2013 Big 12 Conference realignment") left the conference with 10 members, and [later expansions](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932026_NCAA_conference_realignment#Big_12_Conference "2021–2026 NCAA conference realignment") brought the membership totals to 14 in 2023 and 16 effective in 2024. On the other hand, the Pac-12 Conference used names (official or unofficial) that have reflected the number of members from the establishment of its current charter in 1959 until [its collapse in 2024](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932026_NCAA_conference_realignment#Pac-12_Conference "2021–2026 NCAA conference realignment"). The conference unofficially used "Big Five" (1959–62), "Big Six" (1962–64), and "Pacific-8" (1964–68) before officially adopting the "Pacific-8" name. The name duly changed to "Pacific-10" in 1978 with the addition of Arizona and Arizona State, and "Pac-12" (instead of "Pacific-12") in 2011 when [Colorado and Utah joined](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932014_NCAA_conference_realignment#Pac-10 "2010–2014 NCAA conference realignment"). Conferences also tend to ignore their regional names when adding new schools. For example, the Pac-8/10/12 retained its "Pacific" moniker even though its four most recent additions (Arizona, Arizona State, Colorado, Utah) are located in the inland West, and the [original Big East](/wiki/Big_East_Conference_(1979%E2%80%932013) "Big East Conference (1979–2013)") kept its name even after adding schools (either in all sports or for football only) located in areas traditionally considered to be in the Midwest (Cincinnati, DePaul, Marquette, Notre Dame), Upper South (Louisville, Memphis) and Southwest (Houston, SMU). The non-football conference that [assumed the Big East name](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") when the original Big East [split in 2013](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932013_Big_East_Conference_realignment#Catholic_7 "2010–2013 Big East Conference realignment") is another example of this phenomenon, as half of its 10 inaugural schools (Butler, Creighton, DePaul, Marquette, Xavier) are traditionally regarded as being Midwestern. An even more extrema example of this phenomenon is the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference"). For the first 60 years after its 1953 founding, the ACC consisted entirely of schools in [Atlantic Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") states. However, in [2013](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932013_Big_East_Conference_realignment "2010–2013 Big East Conference realignment"), the conference added three new schools, two of which ([Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Panthers "Pittsburgh Panthers") and, for non-football sports, Indiana-based [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish "Notre Dame Fighting Irish")) were in states without an Atlantic shoreline. The following year saw the ACC add another non-Atlantic school in [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville_Cardinals "Louisville Cardinals"). Then, in [2023](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932026_NCAA_conference_realignment#Atlantic_Coast_Conference "2021–2026 NCAA conference realignment"), the conference announced it would expand in 2024 to the *[Pacific](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States")* coast with [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area") rivals [California](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears") and [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal "Stanford Cardinal"), and also add [SMU](/wiki/SMU_Mustangs "SMU Mustangs") from [Dallas–Fort Worth](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex").

### Football Championship Subdivision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Football Championship Subdivision")]

Main article: [NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision")

See also: [NCAA Division I Football Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship") and [List of NCAA Division I FCS football programs](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_I_FCS_football_programs "List of NCAA Division I FCS football programs")

The **Division I Football Championship Subdivision** (**FCS**), formerly known as **Division I-AA**, consists of 130 teams as of the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_NCAA_Division_I_FCS_football_season "2022 NCAA Division I FCS football season"), with all participating in one of 14 conferences.[[77]](#cite_note-158) The "I-AA" designation was dropped by the NCAA in 2006, although it is still informally and commonly used. FCS teams are limited to 63 players on scholarship (compared to 85 for FBS teams) and, through the 2025 season, usually play an 11-game schedule (compared to 12 games for FBS teams).[[78]](#cite_note-159) The FCS regular-season schedule will permanently expand to 12 games, matching FBS, in 2026.[[79]](#cite_note-160) The FCS determines its national champion through an NCAA-sanctioned single-elimination [bracket tournament](/wiki/Bracket_(tournament) "Bracket (tournament)"), culminating in a title game, the [NCAA Division I Football Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship").[[80]](#cite_note-161) As of the 2018 season, the tournament begins with 24 teams; 10 conference champions that received automatic bids, and 14 teams selected [at-large](/wiki/At-large_bid "At-large bid") by a selection committee.[[81]](#cite_note-162)

The postseason tournament traditionally begins on [Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)") weekend in late November. When I-AA was formed 48 years ago in 1978,[[82]](#cite_note-bswb-163) the playoffs included just four teams for its first three seasons, doubling to eight teams for one season in 1981.[[83]](#cite_note-nexeig-164) From 1982 to 1985, there was a 12-team tournament; this expanded to 16 teams in 1986. The playoffs expanded to 20 teams starting in 2010, then grew to 24 teams in 2013. Since the 2010 season, the title game has been held in early January. The 2010 season was the first of a 15-year run for the title game at [Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Toyota_Stadium_(Texas) "Toyota Stadium (Texas)") in [Frisco, Texas](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas"). Due to renovations planned for that stadium, the title game has moved to [FirstBank Stadium](/wiki/FirstBank_Stadium "FirstBank Stadium") on the campus of [Vanderbilt University](/wiki/Vanderbilt_University "Vanderbilt University") in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") for at least the 2025 and 2026 seasons. From 1997 through 2009, the title game was played in December in [Chattanooga, Tennessee](/wiki/Chattanooga,_Tennessee "Chattanooga, Tennessee"), preceded by five seasons in [Huntington, West Virginia](/wiki/Huntington,_West_Virginia "Huntington, West Virginia").[[84]](#cite_note-165)

#### Abstainers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Abstainers")]

The Football Championship Subdivision includes several conferences which do not participate in the eponymous post-season championship tournament.

The [Southwestern Athletic Conference](/wiki/Southwestern_Athletic_Conference "Southwestern Athletic Conference") (SWAC) has its own [championship game](/wiki/SWAC_Championship_Game "SWAC Championship Game") in mid-December between the champions of its East and West divisions. Also, three of its member schools traditionally do not finish their regular seasons until Thanksgiving weekend. [Grambling State](/wiki/Grambling_State_Tigers_football "Grambling State Tigers football") and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Jaguars_football "Southern Jaguars football") play each other in the [Bayou Classic](/wiki/Bayou_Classic "Bayou Classic"), and [Alabama State](/wiki/Alabama_State_Hornets_football "Alabama State Hornets football") plays [Tuskegee](/wiki/Tuskegee_Golden_Tigers_football "Tuskegee Golden Tigers football") (of [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II")) in the [Turkey Day Classic](/wiki/Turkey_Day_Classic "Turkey Day Classic"). SWAC teams are eligible to accept at-large bids if their schedule is not in conflict. The last SWAC team to participate in the I-AA playoffs was [Jackson State](/wiki/Jackson_State_Tigers_football "Jackson State Tigers football") in [1997](/wiki/1997_NCAA_Division_I-AA_football_season#Postseason "1997 NCAA Division I-AA football season"); the SWAC never achieved success in the tournament, going winless in 19 games in twenty years (1978–97). It had greater success outside the conference while in Division II and the preceding College Division.

From 2006 through 2009, the [Pioneer Football League](/wiki/Pioneer_Football_League "Pioneer Football League") and [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") champions played in the [Gridiron Classic](/wiki/Gridiron_Classic_(2006%E2%80%932009) "Gridiron Classic (2006–2009)"). If a league champion was invited to the national championship playoff as an at-large bid (something the Pioneer league, at least, never received), the second-place team would play in the Gridiron Classic. That game was scrapped after the 2009 season when its four-year contract ran out; this coincided with the NCAA's announcement that the Northeast Conference would receive an automatic bid to the tournament starting in 2010. The [Big South Conference](/wiki/Big_South_Conference "Big South Conference") also received an automatic bid in the same season. The Pioneer Football League earned an automatic bid beginning in 2013.

The [Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference](/wiki/Mid-Eastern_Athletic_Conference "Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference") (MEAC) began abstaining from the playoffs with the 2015 season. Like the SWAC, its members are eligible for at-large bids, and the two conferences have faced off in the [Celebration Bowl](/wiki/Celebration_Bowl "Celebration Bowl") as an alternative postseason game since the 2015 season.

Before the 2025 season, the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League"), which had been reclassified to I-AA (FCS) following the [1981 season](/wiki/1981_NCAA_Division_I-A_football_season "1981 NCAA Division I-A football season"),[[85]](#cite_note-166) did not participate in the I-AA/FCS playoffs despite having an automatic bid, citing academic concerns. The conference still plays a strict ten-game schedule, despite the allowed FCS schedule having been 11 games (and 12 in some seasons) since the creation of the subdivision, with a permanent expansion to 12 games following in 2026. The Ivy League had allowed postseason play in all other sports. The last Ivy League member to play in a bowl game was [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Lions_football "Columbia Lions football") in the [1934 Rose Bowl](/wiki/1934_Rose_Bowl "1934 Rose Bowl"). In December 2024, the Ivies abandoned their longstanding ban on football postseason play, and the conference would participate in the FCS playoffs from 2025 on.[[86]](#cite_note-167)[[87]](#cite_note-168)

Schools in a transition period after joining the FCS from a lower division (or from the [NAIA](/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics "National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics")) are also ineligible for the playoffs.

#### Scholarships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Scholarships")]

Division I FCS schools are currently restricted to giving financial assistance amounting to 63 full scholarships. As FCS football is an "equivalency" sport (as opposed to the "head-count" status of FBS football), Championship Subdivision schools may divide their allotment into partial scholarships. However, FCS schools may only have 85 players receiving any sort of athletic financial aid for football—the same numeric limit as FBS schools. Because of competitive forces, however, a substantial number of players in Championship Subdivision programs are on full scholarships. A former difference was that FCS schools had a limit of 30 players that could be provided with financial aid in a given season, while FBS schools were limited to 25 such additions per season. These limits were suspended in 2020 before being completely eliminated for both subdivisions in 2023.[[69]](#cite_note-FBSreq23-128) Finally, FCS schools are limited to 95 individuals participating in preseason practices, as opposed to 105 at FBS schools (the three service academies that play FBS football are exempt from preseason practice player limits by NCAA rule).

A few Championship Subdivision conferences are composed of schools that offer no athletic scholarships at all, most notably the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") and the [Pioneer Football League](/wiki/Pioneer_Football_League "Pioneer Football League") (PFL), a football-only conference. The Ivy League allows no athletic scholarships at all, while the PFL consists of schools that offer scholarships in other sports but choose not to take on the expense of a scholarship football program. The [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") also sponsored non-scholarship football, but began offering a maximum of 30 full scholarship equivalents in 2006, which grew to 40 in 2011 after a later vote of the league's school presidents and athletic directors and has since increased to 45.[[88]](#cite_note-169) The [Patriot League](/wiki/Patriot_League "Patriot League") only began awarding football scholarships in the 2013 season, with the first scholarships awarded only to incoming freshmen. Before the conference began its transition to scholarship football, athletes receiving scholarships in other sports were ineligible to play football for member schools. Since the completion of the transition with the 2016 season, member schools have been allowed up to 60 full scholarship equivalents.[[89]](#cite_note-170)

10. **[^](#cite_ref-180)** The CAA football conference, branded as [CAA Football](/wiki/Coastal_Athletic_Association_Football_Conference "Coastal Athletic Association Football Conference") and legally a separate entity from the all-sports CAA, was only founded in 2007, but has a continuous history dating to the late 1930s (although not under the same charter):
    * The [New England Conference](/wiki/New_England_Conference "New England Conference") was formed by five New England state universities, plus one private university in that region ([Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_University "Northeastern University")), in 1938. However, the CAA does not consider this conference to be a part of the history of CAA Football. Four of the public schools—[Maine](/wiki/University_of_Maine "University of Maine"), [UMass](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/University_of_New_Hampshire "University of New Hampshire"), and [Rhode Island](/wiki/University_of_Rhode_Island "University of Rhode Island")—were in the CAA football conference through the 2011 season. However, [UMass football](/wiki/UMass_Minutemen_football "UMass Minutemen football") left for the MAC in 2012. [URI football](/wiki/Rhode_Island_Rams_football "Rhode Island Rams football") initially planned to leave for the Northeast Conference in 2013, but decided to remain in the CAA.
    * In 1946, the four then-remaining members of the New England Conference affiliated with two other schools to form the [Yankee Conference](/wiki/Yankee_Conference "Yankee Conference") under a separate charter, with athletic competition starting in 1947. The CAA considers its football history to have started with the formation of the Yankee Conference.
    * In 1997, the Yankee Conference was absorbed by the [Atlantic 10 Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_10_Conference "Atlantic 10 Conference"). The A-10 inherited the Yankee Conference's automatic berth in the [Division I-AA (now FCS) playoffs](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship"). In addition to the four charter New England Conference members mentioned above, five other members of the Yankee Conference at the time of the A-10 merger are still in CAA Football.
    * After the 2006 season, all of the A-10 football teams left for the new CAA football conference. CAA Football inherited the A-10's automatic berth in the FCS playoffs.

All-sports competition began in 1955, with the EIBL directly absorbed into the new league.

### Division I non-football schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Division I non-football schools")]

Several Bowl Subdivision and Championship Subdivision conferences have member institutions that do not compete in football. Such schools are sometimes unofficially referred to as I-AAA.[[90]](#cite_note-Division_IAAA-210)

The following non-football conferences have full members that sponsor football:

* The [America East Conference](/wiki/America_East_Conference "America East Conference") has four football-sponsoring schools: [Albany](/wiki/University_at_Albany,_SUNY "University at Albany, SUNY"), [Bryant](/wiki/Bryant_University "Bryant University"), [Maine](/wiki/University_of_Maine "University of Maine"), and [New Hampshire](/wiki/University_of_New_Hampshire "University of New Hampshire"). All play in [CAA Football](/wiki/Coastal_Athletic_Association_Football_Conference "Coastal Athletic Association Football Conference"), the technically separate football league of the [Coastal Athletic Association](/wiki/Coastal_Athletic_Association "Coastal Athletic Association") (CAA).
* The [Atlantic Sun Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Sun_Conference "Atlantic Sun Conference") (ASUN) has seven schools that sponsor football.
  + [Austin Peay](/wiki/Austin_Peay_Governors_football "Austin Peay Governors football"), [Central Arkansas](/wiki/Central_Arkansas_Bears_football "Central Arkansas Bears football"), [Eastern Kentucky](/wiki/Eastern_Kentucky_Colonels_football "Eastern Kentucky Colonels football"), [North Alabama](/wiki/North_Alabama_Lions_football "North Alabama Lions football"), and [West Georgia](/wiki/West_Georgia_Wolves_football "West Georgia Wolves football") play in the [United Athletic Conference](/wiki/United_Athletic_Conference_(football) "United Athletic Conference (football)") (UAC), which started play in 2023 as a football-only merger between the ASUN and the Western Athletic Conference. These schools will all become members of the UAC when it replaces the WAC as a multi-sports conference in 2026.
  + [Stetson](/wiki/Stetson_Hatters_football "Stetson Hatters football") plays in the PFL.
  + [Bellarmine](/wiki/Bellarmine_Knights#Sprint_football "Bellarmine Knights") does not play NCAA football, instead playing the weight-restricted and non-NCAA variant of [sprint football](/wiki/Sprint_football "Sprint football").
* The [Atlantic 10 Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_10_Conference "Atlantic 10 Conference") has six football-sponsoring members:
  + [Davidson](/wiki/Davidson_Wildcats_football "Davidson Wildcats football") and [Dayton](/wiki/Dayton_Flyers_football "Dayton Flyers football") play in the PFL.
  + [Duquesne](/wiki/Duquesne_Dukes_football "Duquesne Dukes football") plays in the NEC (historically the [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference")).
  + [Fordham](/wiki/Fordham_Rams_football "Fordham Rams football") and [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond_Spiders_football "Richmond Spiders football") play in the [Patriot League](/wiki/Patriot_League "Patriot League").
  + [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island_Rams_football "Rhode Island Rams football") plays in CAA Football.
* The current [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") has four football-sponsoring schools. Three play in FCS: [Butler](/wiki/Butler_Bulldogs_football "Butler Bulldogs football") in the PFL, [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_football "Georgetown Hoyas football") in the Patriot League, and [Villanova](/wiki/Villanova_Wildcats_football "Villanova Wildcats football") in CAA Football, with Villanova moving football to the Patriot League for 2026 and beyond. The fourth, [UConn](/wiki/University_of_Connecticut "University of Connecticut"), [plays](/wiki/UConn_Huskies_football "UConn Huskies football") as an FBS independent.
* Three [Big West Conference](/wiki/Big_West_Conference "Big West Conference") members have football programs. [UC Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis") and [Cal Poly](/wiki/California_Polytechnic_State_University "California Polytechnic State University") play FCS football in the [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference"), and [Hawaiʻi](/wiki/University_of_Hawaii_at_Manoa "University of Hawaii at Manoa") [plays FBS football](/wiki/Hawaii_Rainbow_Warriors_football "Hawaii Rainbow Warriors football") in the [Mountain West Conference](/wiki/Mountain_West_Conference "Mountain West Conference") (MW). Hawaiʻi and UC Davis will leave the Big West in 2026 to become a full MW member with UC Davis remaining in the [Big Sky](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference") for football.[[91]](#cite_note-211)
* The [Horizon League](/wiki/Horizon_League "Horizon League") has two football schools. [Robert Morris](/wiki/Robert_Morris_University "Robert Morris University") [plays](/wiki/Robert_Morris_Colonials_football "Robert Morris Colonials football") in the NEC, and [Youngstown State](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University") [plays](/wiki/Youngstown_State_Penguins_football "Youngstown State Penguins football") in the [Missouri Valley Football Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Football_Conference "Missouri Valley Football Conference") (MVFC). It will add a third football school in 2026 with the return of [Northern Illinois](/wiki/Northern_Illinois_University "Northern Illinois University"), which will [play football](/wiki/Northern_Illinois_Huskies_football "Northern Illinois Huskies football") in the MW.
* The [Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference](/wiki/Metro_Atlantic_Athletic_Conference "Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference") (MAAC) has three football schools. [Marist](/wiki/Marist_Red_Foxes_football "Marist Red Foxes football") plays in the PFL, and [Merrimack](/wiki/Merrimack_College "Merrimack College") and [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_University "Sacred Heart University") were FCS independents in the 2025 season. Sacred Heart will join CAA Football in 2026.
* The [Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference") has seven football schools: [Drake](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University"), [Illinois State](/wiki/Illinois_State_University "Illinois State University"), [Indiana State](/wiki/Indiana_State_University "Indiana State University"), [Murray State](/wiki/Murray_State_University "Murray State University"), [Northern Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Iowa "University of Northern Iowa"), [Southern Illinois](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University_Carbondale "Southern Illinois University Carbondale") and [Valparaiso](/wiki/Valparaiso_University "Valparaiso University"). Drake and Valparaiso play in the PFL; all others compete in the MVFC (a separate legal entity from the MVC, despite the similar name).
* [The Summit League](/wiki/Summit_League "Summit League") has five football schools, four of which play in the MVFC: [North Dakota](/wiki/University_of_North_Dakota "University of North Dakota"), [North Dakota State](/wiki/North_Dakota_State_University "North Dakota State University"), [South Dakota](/wiki/University_of_South_Dakota "University of South Dakota"), and [South Dakota State](/wiki/South_Dakota_State_University "South Dakota State University"). The fifth, [St. Thomas](/wiki/University_of_St._Thomas_(Minnesota) "University of St. Thomas (Minnesota)") of Minnesota, plays in the PFL.
* The [West Coast Conference](/wiki/West_Coast_Conference "West Coast Conference")'s (WCC) only football school, [San Diego](/wiki/University_of_San_Diego "University of San Diego"), [plays in the PFL](/wiki/San_Diego_Toreros_football "San Diego Toreros football"). [Oregon State](/wiki/Oregon_State_University "Oregon State University") and [Washington State](/wiki/Washington_State_University "Washington State University"), the only members of the [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference") until the addition of seven new members in 2026–27, are housing most of their non-football sports in the WCC through 2025–26.

Abilene Christian and Tarleton State will remain members through the WAC's July 2026 transition to the United Athletic Conference. Southern Utah and Utah Tech will join the Big Sky Conference at that time.

Of these, the two that most recently sponsored football were the Atlantic 10 and MAAC. The A-10 football league dissolved in 2006 with its members going to CAA Football, the technically separate football league operated by the all-sports Coastal Athletic Association. In addition, three A-10 schools ([Dayton](/wiki/University_of_Dayton "University of Dayton"), [Duquesne](/wiki/Duquesne_University "Duquesne University"), and [Fordham](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University")) play football in a conference other CAA Football, which still includes one full-time A-10 member, [Rhode Island](/wiki/University_of_Rhode_Island "University of Rhode Island"). The MAAC stopped sponsoring football in 2007, after most of its members gradually stopped fielding teams. Among current MAAC members that were in the conference before 2007, only [Marist](/wiki/Marist_University "Marist University"), which plays in the Pioneer Football League, still sponsors football.

From 2013 to 2021, the [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference") was a non-football league, having dropped football after [a near-complete membership turnover](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932013_Western_Athletic_Conference_realignment "2010–2013 Western Athletic Conference realignment") that saw the conference stripped of all but two of its football-sponsoring members. The two remaining football-sponsoring schools, [Idaho](/wiki/University_of_Idaho "University of Idaho") and [New Mexico State](/wiki/New_Mexico_State_University "New Mexico State University"), played the 2013 season as FBS independents before becoming football-only members of the Sun Belt Conference in 2014. Both left Sun Belt football in 2018, with Idaho downgrading to FCS status and adding football to its all-sports Big Sky Conference membership and New Mexico State becoming an FBS independent. The WAC added two more football-sponsoring schools with the 2020 arrival of Tarleton and Utah Tech (then Dixie State) from Division II; both schools planned to be FCS independents for the foreseeable future. The WAC would reinstate football at the FCS level in 2021, coinciding with the arrival of four new members with FCS football;[[92]](#cite_note-229)[[93]](#cite_note-230) for its first season, it entered into a formal partnership with the ASUN Conference to give it enough playoff-eligible members to receive an automatic playoff berth.[[94]](#cite_note-231) This partnership was renewed for the 2022 season, with five ASUN and three WAC schools participating, though each conference will play its own schedule.[[95]](#cite_note-232) After the 2022 season, the ASUN and WAC announced a full football merger for 2023 and beyond under the banner of the [United Athletic Conference](/wiki/United_Athletic_Conference_(football) "United Athletic Conference (football)").[[96]](#cite_note-233)[[97]](#cite_note-234)

In July 2026, the UAC will become a multi-sports conference as a rebranding of the WAC. When this change was announced in June 2025, the UAC membership going forward was to consist of the five current ASUN members that play UAC football, two football-sponsoring WAC members, and non-football legacy WAC member UT Arlington. Later in 2025, the UAC announced that another non-football school, Little Rock, would join the conference in 2026.[[98]](#cite_note-235) The reconfigured ASUN will consist entirely of schools that do not sponsor scholarship FCS football.[[99]](#cite_note-236)

## Division I in ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Division I in ice hockey")]

Main article: [College ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey")

See also: [List of NCAA Division I ice hockey programs](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_I_ice_hockey_programs "List of NCAA Division I ice hockey programs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Cornell_vs._Providence_College_NCAA_ice_hockey.jpg/250px-Cornell_vs._Providence_College_NCAA_ice_hockey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cornell_vs._Providence_College_NCAA_ice_hockey.jpg)

[Providence College Friars](/wiki/Providence_Friars_men%27s_ice_hockey "Providence Friars men's ice hockey") play [Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_Big_Red_men%27s_ice_hockey "Cornell Big Red men's ice hockey") in the NCAA [Hockey East](/wiki/Hockey_East "Hockey East") Regional at the [Dunkin' Donuts Center](/wiki/Dunkin%27_Donuts_Center "Dunkin' Donuts Center"), April 7, 2019

Some sports, most notably [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")[[100]](#cite_note-insidecollegehockey.com-237) and men's [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), have completely different conference structures that operate outside of the normal NCAA sports conference structure.

As [ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey") is limited to a much smaller number of almost exclusively Northern schools, there is a completely different conference structure for teams.[[100]](#cite_note-insidecollegehockey.com-237) These conferences feature a mix of teams that play their other sports in various Division I conferences, and even [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") and [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") schools. For most of the early 21st century, there was no correlation between a team's ice hockey affiliation and its affiliation for other sports, with the exception of the Ivy League's hockey-playing schools all being members of the ECAC. For example, before 2013, the [Hockey East](/wiki/Hockey_East "Hockey East") men's conference consisted of one ACC school, one Big East school, four schools from the America East, one from the A-10, one CAA school, and two schools from the D-II [Northeast-10 Conference](/wiki/Northeast-10_Conference "Northeast-10 Conference"), while the [Central Collegiate Hockey Association](/wiki/Central_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "Central Collegiate Hockey Association") (CCHA) and [Western Collegiate Hockey Association](/wiki/Western_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "Western Collegiate Hockey Association") (WCHA) both had some Big Ten representation, plus Division II and III schools. Also, the divisional structure is truncated, with the Division II championship abolished in 1999.

The [Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference](/wiki/Metro_Atlantic_Athletic_Conference "Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference") ceased its sponsorship of the sport in 2003,[[101]](#cite_note-238) with the remaining members forming [Atlantic Hockey](/wiki/Atlantic_Hockey "Atlantic Hockey"). For the next decade, no regular all-sport conferences sponsored ice hockey.

Starting with the 2013–14 season, Division I men's hockey experienced [a major realignment](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932014_NCAA_conference_realignment#Hockey "2010–2014 NCAA conference realignment"). The [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") began to sponsor ice hockey, and their institutions withdrew their membership from the WCHA and CCHA.[[102]](#cite_note-239) Additionally, six other schools from those conferences withdrew to form the new [National Collegiate Hockey Conference](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Hockey_Conference "National Collegiate Hockey Conference") at the same time.[[103]](#cite_note-240) The fallout from these moves led to the demise of the original CCHA, two more teams entering the NCHC, and further membership turnover in the men's side of the WCHA.[[104]](#cite_note-241)

Women's hockey was largely unaffected by this realignment. The Big Ten still has only four members with varsity women's hockey (full members Michigan and Michigan State only ice men's teams, as does hockey-only member Notre Dame), with six teams required under conference bylaws for official sponsorship. As a result, the only changes in women's hockey affiliations in the 2010–14 period occurred in [College Hockey America](/wiki/College_Hockey_America "College Hockey America"), which saw two schools drop the sport and three new members join.

The next significant realignment took place after the 2020–21 season, when seven of the 10 then-current men's members of the WCHA left to form a revived CCHA,[[105]](#cite_note-242) which in turn led to the demise of the men's side of the WCHA.[[106]](#cite_note-243)

## Classification debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NCAA_Division_I&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Classification debate")]

In the early 21st century, a controversy arose in the NCAA over whether schools will continue to be allowed to have one showcased program in Division I with the remainder of the athletic program in a lower division, as is the case of, notably, [Johns Hopkins University lacrosse](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays_men%27s_lacrosse "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays men's lacrosse") as well as [Colorado College](/wiki/Colorado_College "Colorado College") and [University of Alabama in Huntsville](/wiki/University_of_Alabama_in_Huntsville "University of Alabama in Huntsville") in [ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey"). This is an especially important issue in [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), which has no Division II national championship and has several schools whose other athletic programs compete in Division II and Division III.

This controversy was resolved at the 2004 NCAA Convention in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") when the members supported Proposal 65–1, the amended legislation co-sponsored by [Colorado College](/wiki/Colorado_College "Colorado College"), [Clarkson University](/wiki/Clarkson_University "Clarkson University"), [Hartwick College](/wiki/Hartwick_College "Hartwick College"), the [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), [Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute"), [Rutgers University–Newark](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Newark "Rutgers University–Newark"), [St. Lawrence University](/wiki/St._Lawrence_University "St. Lawrence University"), and [SUNY Oneonta](/wiki/SUNY_Oneonta "SUNY Oneonta").[[107]](#cite_note-255)[[108]](#cite_note-256) Each school affected by this debate is allowed to grant financial aid to student-athletes who compete in Division I programs in one men's sport and one women's sport. It is still permitted for other schools to place one men's and one women's sport in Division I going forward, but they cannot offer scholarships without bringing the whole program into compliance with Division I rules. In addition, schools in Divisions II and III are allowed to "play up" in any sport that does not have a championship for the school's own division, but only Division II programs and any Division III programs covered by the exemption can offer scholarships in those sports.

Five Division I programs at "waiver schools" were grandfathered with the passing of Proposal 65-1:

* [Clarkson University](/wiki/Clarkson_University "Clarkson University") – [men's](/wiki/Clarkson_Golden_Knights_men%27s_ice_hockey "Clarkson Golden Knights men's ice hockey") and [women's ice hockey](/wiki/Clarkson_Golden_Knights_women%27s_ice_hockey "Clarkson Golden Knights women's ice hockey")
* [Colorado College](/wiki/Colorado_College "Colorado College") – [men's ice hockey](/wiki/Colorado_College_Tigers_men%27s_ice_hockey "Colorado College Tigers men's ice hockey"), women's [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer")
* [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University") – [men's](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays_men%27s_lacrosse "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays men's lacrosse") and [women's lacrosse](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays_women%27s_lacrosse "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays women's lacrosse")
* [Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute") – [men's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_men%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers men's ice hockey") ([women's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_women%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers women's ice hockey") moved up to Division I in 2005)
* [St. Lawrence University](/wiki/St._Lawrence_University "St. Lawrence University") – [men's](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Saints_men%27s_ice_hockey "St. Lawrence Saints men's ice hockey") and [women's ice hockey](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Saints_women%27s_ice_hockey "St. Lawrence Saints women's ice hockey")

An additional three programs were grandfathered in Proposal 65-1 but no longer are sponsored in Division I:

* [Hartwick College](/wiki/Hartwick_College "Hartwick College") – men's [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer"), women's [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") (men's soccer dropped to [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") in 2018, with women's water polo discontinued at the same time)
* [Rutgers University–Newark](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Newark "Rutgers University–Newark") – men's [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") (dropped to Division III in 2014)
* [SUNY Oneonta](/wiki/SUNY_Oneonta "SUNY Oneonta") – men's [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer") (dropped to Division III in 2006)
